title: Media and feature queries

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Media and feature queries; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Media and feature queries
sources.claim: Media queries condition styles on media features such as width, orientation, pointer, hover, contrast, color scheme, and motion preference.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Media and feature queries
sources.claim: Media queries condition styles on media features such as width, orientation, pointer, hover, contrast, color scheme, and motion preference.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Media queries condition styles on media features such as width, orientation, pointer, hover, contrast, color scheme, and motion preference.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Navigation changes layout when its content no longer fits, not at a guessed tablet model width. Your goal is to implement and test layout, color-scheme and contrast adaptations. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Flexbox and Grid; Typography, color and visual treatment; Intrinsic responsive grids; Color and contrast. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Media queries condition styles on media features such as width, orientation, pointer, hover, contrast, color scheme, and motion preference. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Device labels are unreliable, viewport width can change, feature combinations matter, and queries do not change DOM semantics. This draft therefore rejects: Media queries should target named devices such as iPhone and desktop.

includes: Add content-driven breakpoints and feature or preference queries as progressive enhancements.
Implement and test layout, color-scheme and contrast adaptations.
excludes: Device labels are unreliable, viewport width can change, feature combinations matter, and queries do not change DOM semantics.

outcomes: Implement and test layout, color-scheme and contrast adaptations.

examples.title: Media and feature queries: concrete case
examples.situation: Navigation changes layout when its content no longer fits, not at a guessed tablet model width.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Navigation changes layout when its content no longer fits, not at a guessed tablet model width.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Choose breakpoints from content and capabilities, not device brands, and test resize, zoom, input, and preference combinations.
examples.result: The result follows because Choose breakpoints from content and capabilities, not device brands, and test resize, zoom, input, and preference combinations.

examples.title: Media and feature queries: boundary case
examples.situation: Device labels are unreliable, viewport width can change, feature combinations matter, and queries do not change DOM semantics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Device labels are unreliable, viewport width can change, feature combinations matter, and queries do not change DOM semantics.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Media queries should target named devices such as iPhone and desktop.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Choose breakpoints from content and capabilities, not device brands, and test resize, zoom, input, and preference combinations.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Media queries should target named devices such as iPhone and desktop.
misconceptions.correction: Choose breakpoints from content and capabilities, not device brands, and test resize, zoom, input, and preference combinations.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Media queries should target named devices such as iPhone and desktop.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Media and feature queries, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Media and feature queries before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and test layout, color-scheme and contrast adaptations. Keep this boundary in view: Device labels are unreliable, viewport width can change, feature combinations matter, and queries do not change DOM semantics.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Media and feature queries using exact CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling terms.
Why is this claim incorrect: Media queries should target named devices such as iPhone and desktop.
Apply checkpoint 'content and capabilities' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Container queries”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
